title: A Perspectival Moral History of the Novel: What LLMs Can and Cannot Tell Us
summary: This episode examines Andrew Piper’s 2025 study on using large language models to extract story morals from thousands of Wikipedia plot summaries of modern novels. The script stays close to Piper’s claims: the project is interpretive rather than taxonomic, uses prompt variation to test model sensitivity, and treats moral extraction as a perspectival workflow shaped by language, archive, model, and researcher. It also marks where broader implications are inferred rather than directly claimed.
citations: Piper, A. (2025). Towards a Perspectival Moral History of the Novel Using LLMs. Journal of Computational Literary Studies. https://doi.org/10.48694/jcls.4168
featured_notes: The paper frames a "many-to-many relationship" among wikiplots across language editions, highlighting perspectival differences in what gets summarized and how.
The project links story morals to narratological schema traditions (for example, Campbell, Frye, Genette, Propp, Thompson) while shifting focus from "what happened" to "why this was told."
Methods note: the prompt asks for a single-sentence moral, then three keywords, with a fallback of NONE if information is insufficient.
Data note: the study uses 9,756 human-written English Wikipedia story summaries of books published since the start of the twentieth century.

title: Everything Is Television: Flow, Attention, and the New Media Attractor
summary: A fidelity-style walkthrough of Derek Thompson’s 2025 essay arguing that major media formats are converging toward continuous short-form video “flow,” with implications for social life, attention, and political culture. The script distinguishes Thompson’s claims, his cited evidence, and clearly labeled derived inferences.
featured_notes: A spooky convergence is happening in media.
Whether the starting point is a student directory (Facebook), radio, or an AI image generator, the end point seems to be the same: a river of short-form video.
In his 1974 book Television: Technology and Cultural Form, Raymond Williams: wrote that “in all communications systems before [television], the essential items were discrete.”
300 hours a year to learn a new skill, or participate in their community, or have more children.
